Commercial Apr 10, 2026

Cluj-Napoca: the rapidly growing Romanian airport that is now eyeing US flights

If there is one airport that has benefited from the momentous change to travel habits in Eastern Europe…...

Cluj-Napoca's strategic positioning in Eastern Europe positions it as a potential transatlantic hub, capitalizing on underserved regional demand and post-pandemic route expansion opportunities.

See AeroTime Hub →

Commercial Apr 10, 2026

At Maximum Range, This Is What Pushes Aircraft Like The Airbus A350 To Their Limits

Pushing the Airbus A350 to its maximum range is a risk, but for the furtherance of aviation history, it is likely one worth taking.

Operating aircraft at absolute maximum range significantly increases fuel-margin risk and structural stress, making it operationally impractical despite being technically feasible for record attempts.

See Simple Flying →

Military Apr 9, 2026

From 19 B-2 Spirits To 145 B-21 Raiders: How Much Bigger America's Stealth Bomber Fleet Is About To Get

A $4.5 billion production deal signal the Air Force's commitment to building its next-generation bomber...

The Air Force's shift from 19 B-2s to 145 B-21s represents a 660% fleet expansion, fundamentally reshaping long-range strike capability and defense spending priorities for decades.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Asiana Airlines to be Taken Over by Korean Air 2026

According to local news outlet Tomato News, Korean Air is planning to acquire the remaining 36.1% stake in Asiana Airlines. It owns 63.9% currently and much control in the management of it. But it wants to form a single airline structure, gradually removing the airline as a brand, starting in...

Korean Air's acquisition of Asiana will consolidate South Korea's two largest carriers into a single operational entity, reducing domestic capacity competition while potentially improving network efficiency.

See Travel Radar →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Federal Aviation Administration's Crippling Shortage Risks Mass Flight Delays & Safety

More than 5,100 accidents had a narrative that air traffic control was a factor or probable cause.

With ATC implicated in over 5,100 accident narratives, the FAA's staffing crisis directly correlates to documented safety risks beyond operational delays.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Safety Apr 10, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS transforms aviation safety from incident-driven responses to predictive risk management, reducing accidents before they occur.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Airbus Delivered 60 New Planes In March, But Overall Q1 Output Lags 16% From Last Year

Pratt & Whitney engine delays are now the clearest drag on Airbus’ 2026 delivery performance.

Pratt & Whitney's engine supply constraints have become the primary production bottleneck limiting Airbus' ability to meet 2026 delivery targets, despite maintaining steady aircraft assembly rates.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Ouch: American Airlines Joins 4 Other US Carriers In Raising Checked Bag Fees

American Airlines becomes the latest US carrier to increase checked bag fees for basic economy tickets.

Coordinated fee increases across major carriers suggest industry-wide strategy to offset fuel and operational costs while pressuring budget-conscious passengers toward premium cabin purchases.

See Simple Flying →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

Beech Bash to feature aerobatic Bonanza reunion

Organizers expect up to a third of the roughly 30 aerobatic Beechcraft Bonanzas still in service will gather in Madisonville, Kentucky, April 23 to 26 for the fifth annual Beech Bash in the Bluegrass. A long weekend of camaraderie, food, concerts, airshows, and presentations by well-known aviators...

The aerobatic Bonanza community remains vibrant with approximately 30 modified aircraft still actively flying, indicating sustained demand for performance upgrades in the general aviation market.

See AOPA News →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Growth of Regional Airlines this 2026

Regional carriers are gaining traction as major airlines cut capacity or reshuffle their networks due to U.S.–Iran conflict and the surge in fuel prices. This is creating opportunities for smaller operators to serve underserved markets with short‑haul flights. New Routes and Network Expansion in...

Regional carriers are capitalizing on capacity gaps and network disruptions from major airlines, positioning themselves to profitably serve thin routes that larger operators are abandoning.

See Travel Radar →

PAI
Safety Apr 10, 2026

Selecting Risk Controls and Accepting Residual Risk

Where Safety Risk Management moves from analysis to action — how organizations develop controls under §5.55(c)–(d).

Effective safety risk management requires organizations to systematically document and justify why residual risks remain acceptable after implementing feasible controls, not just identify hazards.

See PAI Consulting — SMS Quick Takes →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Why Airlines Prefer Window Shades To Be Open During Taxi, Takeoff, & Landing

This is a simple safety concept, but not all airlines agree.

Open window shades enable cabin crew and passengers to detect external hazards and assist emergency evacuations, making it a critical but inconsistently enforced safety protocol across carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

Windshaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's NUARC facility enables controlled wind testing for autonomous aircraft development without weather delays, accelerating certification pathways for emerging autonomous platforms.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

I Am Artemis: Dan Florez

Listen to this audio excerpt from Dan Florez, test director for NASA’s Exploration Ground Systems Program: At 1:47 a.m. EST November 16, 2022, as the Artemis I engines ignited, NASA astronaut Reid Wiseman and Dan Florez, NASA test director for the agency’s Exploration Ground Systems, watched from...

Ground systems test directors like Florez are critical mission enablers whose real-time operational decisions directly determine launch success for deep-space exploration programs.

See NASA →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Frontier Airlines Pilots Slam Brakes On Busy LAX Taxiway: “2 Trucks Just Cut Us Off”

The near-miss has sparked concerns about vehicle transponders, following the deadly Air Canada crash last month.

The incident reveals a critical vulnerability in ground operations where vehicle transponders may not be universally equipped or monitored on busy taxiways, creating blind spots despite modern avionics.

See Simple Flying →

▶
General Aviation Apr 10, 2026

From the Flight Deck – Eastern Iowa Airport, IA (CID)

Eastern Iowa Airport (CID) is a medium-sized multi-use airport located south of Cedar Rapids, Iowa. Traffic includes basic training aircraft up through air carrier aircraft. Flight schools often use...

Medium-sized regional airports like CID serve as crucial training hubs that bridge general aviation and commercial operations, creating a mixed-use environment that maximizes facility utilization across diverse aircraft types.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

The Airbus A350 Is Known As The World's Extra Widebody, But Soon It Won’t Be

The Boeing 777X is set to be the widest passenger jet in production, although the A350-1000 appears set to retain the record for maximum exit limit.

Boeing's 777X will surpass the A350 in fuselage width, reshaping wide-body competition and forcing airlines to reassess cabin configuration economics.

See Simple Flying →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Indoor Testing Facilities available at the NASA Unmanned Autonomy Research Complex (NUARC)

WindShaper A large WindShaper fan array is available for dynamic low-speed and hovering flight research. The WindShaper is ideal for generating arbitrary wind gradients and wind gusts via a simple Python API. A companion WindProbe is also available for quick surveys of flows. The WindProbe utilizes...

NASA's NUARC WindShaper enables researchers to simulate complex wind conditions indoors, accelerating autonomous flight validation without outdoor weather delays.

See NASA →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

Only 238 Seats: What Qantas Sacrificed To Make 22-Hour Nonstop Flights Possible

Project Sunrise flights are coming soon.

Qantas stripped passenger capacity by roughly 40% to achieve ultra-long-range operations, signaling that ultra-premium positioning trumps volume economics on emerging mega-routes.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Apr 9, 2026

United Airlines increases checked bag fees

United Airlines has become the latest airline to increase the checked baggage fee, effective immediately, in response to rising fuel costs. Other airlines, including Delta Air Lines, Qantas, and Air Canada, have also increased ticket prices to manage fuel shortages. Check-in luggage cost in United...

Rising fuel costs are driving an industrywide shift toward ancillary revenue strategies, with checked bag fees becoming a primary mechanism to offset operational expenses without visibly raising base fares.

See Travel Radar →

▶
Safety Apr 10, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway incursion risks at LAX persist at high-traffic intersections where pilot confusion during exits can create collision hazards, requiring enhanced ground control procedures.

See Federal Aviation Administration (YouTube) →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

EAA Aviation Foundation Awards 127 Scholarships for 2025-26

Flight training and post-secondary awards total more than $800,000.

With over $800,000 in flight training scholarships, EAA is addressing the industry's critical pilot shortage by making advanced training accessible to 127 candidates.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Starstruck

The Artemis II crew captured this photo of our galaxy, the Milky Way, on April 7, 2026. The Milky Way’s elegant spiral structure is dominated by just two arms wrapping off the ends of a central bar of stars. Spanning more than 100,000 light-years, Earth is located along one of the galaxy’s spiral...

Artemis II's deep-space imagery demonstrates advanced photographic capabilities essential for long-duration lunar missions and future Mars exploration planning.

See NASA →

General Aviation Apr 9, 2026

Picture of the Day: April 9, 2026

2015 Lockwood AirCam over the Palisades Reservoir.

The Lockwood AirCam's enduring presence in recreational flying demonstrates sustained market viability for high-wing, observation-focused aircraft designs.

See AVweb →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

House Set To Vote on ALERT Act Next Week

Measure would direct FAA rulemaking on collision-avoidance systems, airspace procedures and military operations.

Congressional mandate for FAA rulemaking on collision avoidance and airspace procedures could accelerate safety standards but may create timeline conflicts with ongoing regulatory efforts.

See AVweb →

Industry Apr 10, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across airlines, airports, and flight systems can simultaneously expand capacity and reduce environmental impact, addressing the dual challenge of growing demand and sustainability pressures.

See MDPI →

Military Apr 9, 2026

US Navy Pilots In Training Will Now Make Their 1st Carrier Landing In An F/A-18 Or F-35

No tailhook required to earn wings of gold?

Eliminating the tailhook requirement for initial carrier landings accelerates pilot certification timelines while reducing training aircraft attrition and maintenance costs.

See Simple Flying →

Safety Apr 9, 2026

Justice wants $110.3M to boost zero-trust cybersecurity in fiscal 2027

After falling victim to the SolarWinds breach in 2020, Justice has worked to implement a zero-trust cybersecurity architecture, but saw its funding for cyber cut by $108 million in fiscal 2024.

Aviation operators should prioritize zero-trust cybersecurity architecture investments now, as the Justice Department's experience demonstrates that cyber budget cuts create multi-year recovery costs far exceeding initial savings.

See FedScoop →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching the Artemis II Mission Unfold at JPL’s Space Flight Operations Facility

Description Staff at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California watch the agency’s Artemis II mission unfold soon after launch on April 1, 2026, at the Space Flight Operations Facility, which operates the Deep Space Network (DSN). The DSN comprises of three complexes in Goldstone,...

NASA's Deep Space Network infrastructure at JPL demonstrates how ground-based communications systems are critical for managing lunar missions beyond direct control from launch facilities.

See NASA →

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description A graphical representation of the Deep Space Network’s radio frequency antennas indicate signal acquisition from NASA’s Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026, inside the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California. Two antennas...

The Deep Space Network's successful signal acquisition demonstrates critical ground infrastructure capabilities essential for sustained lunar exploration missions beyond Earth orbit.

See NASA →

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

FAA Pilot Population Climbs Again In 2025

Latest Civil Airmen Statistics show the strongest gains from student pilots.

Student pilot growth indicates renewed interest in aviation careers, potentially easing the pilot shortage that has constrained regional airline hiring.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

The Deep Space Network Acquires Artemis II Signal

Description The acquisition of the radio frequency signal from the Artemis II crewed mission to the Moon by NASA’s Deep Space Network (DSN) is indicated by the peak in the data signal shown on the top computer screen. Soon after the mission’s launch on April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT, NASA’s Near...

NASA's Deep Space Network successfully established communication with Artemis II immediately post-launch, validating critical infrastructure for sustained lunar mission operations and beyond.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching Over the Deep Space Network Before Artemis II Signal Acquisition

Description Blanca Renteria, Artemis Deep Space Network (DSN) operations chief, monitors data at the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California shortly after Artemis II launched from the agency’s Kennedy Space Flight Center in Florida on April 1,...

NASA's Deep Space Network operations teams must maintain real-time signal acquisition protocols across distributed ground stations to support crewed lunar missions, requiring aerospace communication infrastructure coordination.

Regulatory Apr 9, 2026

AI risk management deadline hits federal agencies. Not all were ready.

DHS and DOJ are among the agencies lagging behind those that have publicly posted updated AI inventories, per OMB requirements, and outlined changes to FedScoop.

Aviation regulators may face delays in AI governance implementation if federal agencies lag in meeting OMB AI risk management deadlines, potentially affecting certification and oversight timelines.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

Watching the Artemis II Launch From JPL’s Space Flight Operations Facility

Soon after launch, the Artemis II crew communicated...

JPL's Deep Space Network will serve critical communication and tracking functions for Artemis II, extending NASA's operational capabilities beyond traditional launch facilities to support lunar missions.

Aerospace Apr 9, 2026

JPL’s ‘Lucky Peanuts’ Before Artemis II Launch

Description A container of “lucky peanuts” sits above workstations within the Space Flight Operations Facility at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory shortly before the launch of the Artemis II mission to the Moon on April 1, 2026. Eating peanuts before launches and other major mission events is a...

NASA's JPL maintains a decades-old superstition of consuming peanuts before critical missions, reflecting how even highly technical space operations incorporate ritualistic confidence-building practices.
